algorithm_comprehension.notes: Continuous Batching is a scheduling algorithm, not a tiled matrix algorithm — tiling visualization and induction proofs are not the right assessment framework here. The chapter provides: (a) step-by-step numerical trace in Cell 7 with concrete values (B=512, 3 requests), tracking every intermediate state per step; (b) algebraic bubble derivation from first principles (Cell 3-4); (c) restaurant kitchen analogy that maps scheduling concepts to everyday experience. First-time-reader test: a student can draw the two-phase scheduling loop on a whiteboard and trace Steps 1-3 on paper. The bubble diagram (diagram_bubble.png) visualizes the static-vs-CB comparison clearly.

code_walkthrough.notes: Cell 5 covers all 5 sub-sections of schedule(): method initialization (L258-L275), Phase 1 running (L277-L350), Phase 2 waiting (L352-L418), SchedulerOutput construction (L421-L434), plus _preempt_request (L436-L460) and update_after_step (L462-L515) in Cell 6. ALL line numbers verified against the current implementation file and are correct. Each sub-section includes: code block → numbered line-by-line explanation → vLLM diff comparison. Demo output matches actual python3 run. 14 tests pass. The previous review flagged incorrect line numbers — all have been corrected.

issues.description: Inline references use 'scheduler.py:LXXX' without the 'vllm/' prefix. The Cell 5 Source Trail opens with the full path (vllm/v1/core/sched/scheduler.py:L371), which primes the reader, but a newcomer skimming code blocks alone might not know whether 'scheduler.py' refers to the implementation or vLLM source. Consider adding a one-line note at the start of Cell 5: 'Below, scheduler.py without prefix = our implementation; vllm/... = production code.'

chapter_structure.notes: Chapter ID 04-continuous-batching is unique. Content correctly scoped to CB — chunked prefill is a section within, not standalone. Cell numbering: 2→3→4→5→6→7→9→10→11 (skips 1 and 8 — acceptable, established by writer). Cross-chapter references to Ch5 and Ch7 resolve correctly.

source_grounding.notes: Linter: 'All grounding checks passed!'. Every Cell (2-7) has explicit vLLM file:line references. Source Mapping Table in Cell 9 has 11 rows covering all major classes. Implementation file has # REFERENCE: comments on every function (verified by grep — 30+ references). impl-notes.md lists 7 source files with line ranges. The acid test passes: a reader can open the vLLM repo and find scheduler.py:L352, request.py:L310, kv_cache_manager.py, etc.

formula_renderability.notes: Linter results: 0 blocking issues. No \text{}, \boxed{}, \tag*{}, inline \frac, or one-line $$ found. The previous blocking issue (\text{ 步} on old line 102) has been fixed. 21 non-blocking warnings: 11 paragraphs with high inline formula density (3-7 per paragraph) and 10 complex inline formulas (34-108 chars). These are quality warnings, not blocking. The formulas render correctly; the recommendations are about readability optimization.

issues.description: 21 non-blocking formula lint warnings across the chapter: 11 paragraphs with 3-7 inline formulas each, and 10 complex inline formulas (e.g., '\sum(P_i + O_i) = (800 + 200 + 50) + (20 + 50 + 100) = 1050 ...' at 72 chars). These are readability optimizations — consider promoting complex numerical computations to block formulas for visual clarity. Not blocking: all formulas use \mathrm{} (not \text{}), no boxed/tag/frac-in-inline violations.

The Budget label clarification ('等等——这里有个细节...这里的标签名是误导') is meta-text about the chapter's own demo output formatting. It's correct and honest, but reading it feels like watching the author debug in real-time. Consider fixing the demo code to print 'Used: 28/512' instead of explaining the misleading label — cleaner for the reader.
